coredata.dc:title: Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jian
Li, Ya
Lv, Kun
Zhong, Weibang
Wang, Hong
Wu, Zhan
Yi, Pinggui
Jiang, Jianhui
coredata.dc:description: The detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and sulfide anion (S2−) is of vital importance since the abnormal level of Cu2+ or S2− can lead to many diseases. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor, 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (Cyclam)-functionalized carbon dots (CCDs), has been designed, synthesized and evaluated for Cu2+ and S2−. For this nanoprobe, a specific fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process can be effectively take place between carbon dots and the surface Cu2+-Cyclam complex, the as-prepared CCDs display high sensitivity (detection limit: 100nM) and selectivity toward Cu2+ among many other metal cations (such as Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+) in 100% aqueous solution. Moreover, it is worth to point out that the subsequent addition of S2− can extract Cu2+ from the CCDs-Cu2+ complex and recover the fluorescence of carbon dots, the detection limit for S2− can reach to 130nM in the aqueous medium. And no statistically significant interference was observed among the other 10 anions (HCO3 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, F−, Br−, HPO4 −, ClO4 −) for S2− through the study. In addition, the novel type of multifunctional fluorescent sensor has a relatively wide pH range (pH 4–10). At the same time, it exhibited remarkable longterm fluorescence stability (≥35 days) for Cu2+ detection. In addition, this nanoprobe exhibits very low cytotoxicity and can easily permeate the cell membrane and realize Cu2+ and S2− monitoring and imaging in live cells. Therefore, this novel approach can be used in various fields, such as the detection of multiplex analytes in biological applications and environment, and it will reveal great application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyclam
Carbon dots
FRET
Copper ion
Sulfide anion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective fluorescence recognition of Cu2+ in water
Fluorescence turn-on sensing of S2− in water
Investigation of pH-dependence and longterm photostability of senor
Cytotoxicity and the Imaging of copper ion and sulfide ion in live cells
Test the practicability of sensor in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of novel CCDs for Cu2+ and S2− selective recognition.
AFM topography image of the CCDs sample (CCD-3) with the height profile along the line in the topographic image.

(A) Absorption spectra of CCDs sensor (sample: CCD-3) upon addition of Cu(NO3)2 in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water: [Cu(NO3)2]=0μM to 6.4×102 μM. (B) Relationship between absorbance of a CCDs-Cu2+ complex at 525nm and Cu2+ concentration (A 0 and A are the absorbance of sample CCD-3 at 525nm in the absence and in the presence of Cu2+).

(A) Fluorescence spectra (λ ex =370nm) of CCDs sample (CCD-3) upon addition of Cu(NO3)2 in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water: [Cu(NO3)2]=0–40μM; (B) The plot of F/F 0 versus Cu2+ concentration for the CCD-3 sample, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of sample (CCD-3) at 460nm in the absence and in the presence of Cu2+, λ ex =370nm, λ em =460nm.

(A) Fluorescence intensity changes (F/F 0) of CCDs (CCD-3) in complex versus various metal ions in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water (Cu2+ and other cations concentration: 5μM); (B) The influence of various coexisting metal ions on selective sensing of Cu2+ by CCDs in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water (CCD-3) (other competitive ion concentration: 5μM; BLK is only 5μM Cu2+, λ ex =370nm, λ em =460nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble (adding 20μM of Cu2+ to dilute CCD-3 dispersion) upon addition of S2− ([S2−]=0–2.0×10−5M, λ ex =370nm) in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water; (B) The plot of F/F 0 versus S2− concentration for the CCD-3 sample, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of sample CCD-3 at 460nm in the absence and in the presence of S2−, λ ex =370nm, λ em =460nm.

(A) Fluorescence response of CCDs-Cu2+ in the presence of anions in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water (the concentration of all anions and concentration of Cu2+ is 20μM, λ ex =370nm, λ em =460nm). (B) The influence of various coexisting anions on anti-interference of S2− by CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water (BLK is 5μM Cu2 and 20μM S2−, concentration of other anions are 5μM, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of sample CCD-3 at 460nm in the absence and in the presence of S2−, λ ex =370nm, λ em =460nm).

Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with CCDs sensor (CCD-3, 50μgml−1, A and D) and treated with 50μM (B and E) and 100μM (C and F) of Cu2+. The overlay images confirm the signals are from CCDs in cell, not interference. Scale bar, 10μm.

Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with CCDs sensor (CCD-3, 50μgml−1) and 100μM of Cu2+ (A and D), then treated with 75μM (B and E) and 120μM of Cu2+ (C and F). The overlay images confirm the signals are from CCDs in cell, not interference. Scale bar, 10μm.
Some structural and spectral parameters of three CCDs samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The material of CDs and 2-Bromoethyamine is 50/51.2mg, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The accessible Cyclam contents in the CCDs, determined by spectrophotometric titration.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The experimental energy transfer efficiency after adding of 20μM of Cu2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The fluorescence intensity of the four materials of CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of copper ion in blood serum and tap water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: In the test solutions, tap water was ten-fold diluted. Blood serum was firstly mixed with four-fold ethanol and stirred for 5h. Then, the soluble protein in it was removed by centrifugation (10,000rpm/min), and the supernatant blood serum solution was 200-fold diluted before used. The measurements were conducted instantly after the addition of Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, 411201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, 411201, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive (detection limit: 100nM for Cu2+ and 130nM for S2−) and selective detection of Cu2+ and S2− in water were prepared by surface modification of prepared carbon dots. The prepared dual-ion sensor can be reversibly switched for several times by selective addition of adequate Cu2+ or S2−, and exhibited excellent longterm photostability for Cu2+ detection (≥35 days) in aqueous media. More importantly, the CCDs sensor also displayed low cytotoxicity and can be utilized to monitor Cu2+ and S2− level change in live cells.

The detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and sulfide anion (S2−) is of vital importance since the abnormal level of Cu2+ or S2− can lead to many diseases. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor, 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (Cyclam)-functionalized carbon dots (CCDs), has been designed, synthesized and evaluated for Cu2+ and S2−. For this nanoprobe, a specific fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process can be effectively take place between carbon dots and the surface Cu2+-Cyclam complex, the as-prepared CCDs display high sensitivity (detection limit: 100nM) and selectivity toward Cu2+ among many other metal cations (such as Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+) in 100% aqueous solution. Moreover, it is worth to point out that the subsequent addition of S2− can extract Cu2+ from the CCDs-Cu2+ complex and recover the fluorescence of carbon dots, the detection limit for S2− can reach to 130nM in the aqueous medium. And no statistically significant interference was observed among the other 10 anions (HCO3 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, F−, Br−, HPO4 −, ClO4 −) for S2− through the study. In addition, the novel type of multifunctional fluorescent sensor has a relatively wide pH range (pH 4–10). At the same time, it exhibited remarkable longterm fluorescence stability (≥35 days) for Cu2+ detection. In addition, this nanoprobe exhibits very low cytotoxicity and can easily permeate the cell membrane and realize Cu2+ and S2− monitoring and imaging in live cells. Therefore, this novel approach can be used in various fields, such as the detection of multiplex analytes in biological applications and environment, and it will reveal great application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: The study of copper(II) ion (Cu2+) and sulfide anion (S2−) have gained considerable attention in recent years due to their crucial roles in a wide range of biological processes [1–5]. It is well known that (Cu2+) is the third most abundant transition metal ions after iron and zinc in the life system. Cu2+ is also quite vital to the electron transfer processes of various biological reactions in the human body [3,6]. Nevertheless, lack of Cu2+ can affect enzyme activity, leading to a wide variety of neurological problems in the body, while the excess of Cu2+ is related to damage the kidneys and liver [7–9]. On the other hand, S2− exist widely in the environment through a variety of ways, such as it can be achieved from biological metabolism and industrial processes [4,10–13]. As a known toxic pollutant, the abnormal level of S2− will threaten the normal function of life. Moreover, once the S2− turns into HS−, sulfide will become even more toxic for our life [14,15]. The abnormal level of S2− has been also associated with numerous diseases, such as liver damage and Alzheimer's disease [16]. Therefore, developing effective strategies enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and S2− are of great importance to the treatment and environmental requirements.

Compared with other traditional detection technologies, such as electrochemical method [17], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [18] and ion chromatography [19], etc., combination of fluorescence spectroscopy and specific fluorescent sensors to detect Cu2+ and S2− reveals a promising way for its real-time, economic, non-invasive, as well as highly sensitive and selective properties. To date, various sensors have been fabricated for the detection of Cu2+ and S2− [2,11,13]. Among those sensors, carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, have attracted substantial attentions in recent years due to several advantages like tunable surface functionalities, high water solubility, favorable biocompatibility and photostability [20–22]. However, the number of analytes is very limited for the pristine CDs [21–25]. To further broaden their applications in recognition field, CDs modified with additional functional groups are quite important [26–33].

In this study, a novel 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (Cyclam)-functionalized CDs (CCDs) were firstly synthesized for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and S2−. For this sensor, effective Cu2+ ligand (1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane, Cyclam) was chosen to chemically graft onto the surface of CDs. The Cyclam-Cu2+ complex (acceptor) has a specific fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect on carbon dots (donor), when the Cyclam displays high Cu2+-binding affinities [34]. Considering the S2− can effectively combine with Cu2+ to form a stable CuS species with low-solubility (K sp =1.27×10−36) [35], the reaction between the two species can lead to the fluorescence enhancement of in situ generated CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble. Thus, the prepared Cu2+ nanoprobe can also be used as an effective off-on-type sensor for recognition of S2−. In addition, the CCDs also exhibit relatively wide pH applicable range (pH 4–10), attractive longterm fluorescence stability (>35 days) in water, low cytotoxicity and can realize visualized monitoring of Cu2+ and S2− in live cells which argues well for their promising prospects in environmental monitoring and biological applications. Moreover, as exhibited in Table S1, it illustrates comparison of various representative CDs-based methods for Cu2+ or/and S2− detection, suggesting our sensing system exhibits good sensitivity and other unique merits as compared with previously reported sensing systems [2,24,28–33].

In this study, a novel 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (Cyclam)-functionalized CDs (CCDs) were firstly synthesized for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and S2−. For this sensor, effective Cu2+ ligand (1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane, Cyclam) was chosen to chemically graft onto the surface of CDs. The Cyclam-Cu2+ complex (acceptor) has a specific fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect on carbon dots (donor), when the Cyclam displays high Cu2+-binding affinities [34]. Considering the S2− can effectively combine with Cu2+ to form a stable CuS species with low-solubility (K sp =6.3×10−36) [35], the reaction between the two species can lead to the fluorescence enhancement of in situ generated CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble. Thus, the prepared Cu2+ nanoprobe can also be used as an effective off-on-type sensor for recognition of S2−. In addition, the CCDs also exhibit relatively wide pH applicable range (pH 4–10), attractive longterm fluorescence stability (>35 days) in water, low cytotoxicity and can realize visualized monitoring of Cu2+ and S2− in live cells which argues well for their promising prospects in environmental monitoring and biological applications. Moreover, as exhibited in Table S1, it illustrates comparison of various representative CDs-based methods for Cu2+ or/and S2− detection, suggesting our sensing system exhibits good sensitivity and other unique merits as compared with previously reported sensing systems [2,24,28–33].

Citric acid monohydrate (99.5%, Aldrich), ethylenediamine (99%, Aldrich), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%, Aldrich), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.5%, Aldrich), 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (Cyclam, 98%, Aldrich), 2-Bromoethylamine hydrobromide (98%, Aldrich), sodium hexametaphosphate (96%, Aldrich), were used as received. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99%, Aldrich) was purified by vacuum distillation. The water was used in this work is the double-distilled water which was further purified by the Milli-Q system. Other reagents were used without further purification, which are analytical reagents. Nitrate salts of metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+) were of analytic grade. Various anions were used in the experiment were also of analytic grade, which are HCO3 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, F−, Br−, HPO4 − and ClO4 −.

Firstly, CDs were synthesized via a modified microwave-assisted method [32]. Citric acid monohydrate (1.000g) and ethylenediamine (0.346g, 385μL) were added to 10mL of water in a microwave reactor, heated for 5min and the power of the microwave reactor was 80%. After the reaction, the reactants were dropped in 30mL ethanol to purify the mixture. Then the mixture solution was centrifuged (13,000r/s, 10min) and dried by vacuum rotary evaporation at 45°C. Then the product was obtained as a yellow solid (0.800g, 80%).

CDs (0.50g) were dispersed in 25mL of water, then N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.29g, 0.25mmol) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.25g, 0.25mmol) were dissolved in 25ml water and added into the solution. After 1h, 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide (0.51g, 0.25mmol) was dissolved in 10ml water and was added into the solution. Then, the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 48h. The mixture was then removed by dialysis through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 500) toward the pure water for 36h. Then, the halobenzyl-functionalized CDs were obtained. After that, a certain amount of Cyclam was added to 10ml of the crude halobenzyl-functionalized CDs solution (Table 1 ). The solution was stirred at room temperature for 7 days. The excess of Cyclam was then removed by dialysis through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 500) toward the pure water for 36h. Stable Cyclam-functionalized CDs dispersion were obtained and the fluorescence intensity of the Cyclam-functionalized CDs have been tested.

CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay was purchased from Promega (Madison, WI, USA). Cell culture media was purchased from Thermo Scientific HyClone (MA, USA). HeLa cells which were obtained from the cell bank at Xiangya Hospital (Changsha, China), were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 100U/mL of penicillin-streptomycin and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The cells were grown at 37°C in an incubator with a 5% CO2, humidified atmosphere.

The Cytotoxicity of CCDs sensor on HeLa cells proliferation was performed via the CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay. Firstly, HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 5.0×103 cells per well in 96-well plates in three replicates and incubated overnight in 200μL of complete medium. Then, HeLa cells were treated with the prepared CCDs sensor of a given concentration of Cu2+ (0μgmL−1, 50μgmL−1, 100μgmL−1, 200μgmL−1, 300μgmL−1) in 200μL RPMI 1640 medium at 37°C for 24h. The imaging of copper ion about CCDs sensor (50μgmL−1) was evaluated with various concentration of Cu2+ (0μM, 50μM, 100μM) at 37°C under 5% CO2 for 1.5h. And the imaging of S2− about CCDs sensor was evaluated with the same concentration of Cu2+ (100μM) and various concentration of S2− (0μM, 75μM, 120μM) under the same conditions. In the study, HeLa cells were incubated in RPMI1640 medium supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen), and all fluorescence images were acquired using an oil immersion objective (100×, NA 1.3) on a confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope setup containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope with an Olympus FV1000 confocal scanning system.

The diameter of CDs was obtained with an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) in the tapping mode. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2501PC spectrophotometer at room temperature. Fluorescence lifetime (τ) was measured on an Edinburgh FLS920 (UK) fluorescence spectrometer at ambient temperature (298K) and data analysis was observed by simple tail fit method and fitted with a monoexponential decay function. The goodness of fit was estimated by using χ 2 values (between 1.0 and 1.2) in the experiment.

The detection limit of CCDs was calculated by the fluorescence titration. The emission intensity of the CCDs sample (or the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble) without any Cu2+ (or S2−) was measured by 10 times to determine the S/N ratio, and then the standard deviation was determined by the blank measurements. Finally, the detection limit can be calculated by the following equation: detection limit=3σ bi/m, where σ bi is the standard deviation of the blank measurements, and m is the slope between intensity versus Cu2+ (or S2−) concentration.

In this work, CCDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted method and subsequent surface modification technique (Scheme S1). Firstly, a solution containing the reactants (citric acid monohydrate, ethylenediamine) were dispersed into water and heated by microwave reactor for 5min to obtain the carboxyl capped CDs. Then, 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide was coupled with the carboxyl group on the surface of CDs to get halobenzyl-functionalized CDs. Subsequently, Cyclam-functionalized CDs were facilely obtained by adding Cyclam to the halobenzyl-functionalized CDs solution (Scheme S1). Furthermore, we also tried to synthesize CCDs by straightforward strategy via adopting hydrothermal method [28]. However, the fluorescence of as-prepared CDs exhibited little change after adding sufficient Cu2+ (Table S2), which may due to the Cyclam cannot be effectively incorporated onto the surface of prepared CDs. The as-prepared CCDs were quenched upon binding of Cu2+, and recovered by addition of S2−, which reveal a highly sensitive and selective sensing for Cu2+ and S2− in aqueous media, as depicted in Scheme 1 .

In this work, CCDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted method and subsequent surface modification technique (Scheme S1). Firstly, a solution containing the reactants (citric acid monohydrate, ethylenediamine) were dispersed into water and heated by microwave reactor for 5min to obtain the carboxyl capped CDs. Then, 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide was coupled with the carboxyl group on the surface of CDs to get halobenzyl-functionalized CDs. Subsequently, Cyclam-functionalized CDs were facilely obtained by adding Cyclam to the halobenzyl-functionalized CDs solution (Scheme S1). Furthermore, we also tried to synthesize CCDs by straightforward strategy via adopting hydrothermal method [28]. However, the fluorescence of as-prepared CDs exhibited little change after adding sufficient Cu2+ (Table S2), which may due to the Cyclam cannot be effectively incorporated onto the surface of prepared CDs. The as-prepared CCDs were quenched upon binding of Cu2+, and recovered by addition of S2−, which reveal a highly sensitive and selective sensing for Cu2+ and S2− in aqueous media, as depicted in Scheme 1.

In this experiment, as exhibited in Fig. S1, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs shows maximum absorption peak at 352nm, and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs displays a typical excitation-dependent feature as showed in Fig. S2. These results confirm the successful preparation of desirable CDs. The diameter of CCDs was determined by an atomic force microscope (AFM) in the tapping mode. Fig. 1 displays the AFM image for a typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1), which shows that most of the CCDs were discrete, monodisperse, and tiny dots. The cross-sectional view of the CCDs by AFM given in Fig. 1 suggests that the average thickness of CDs is around 2nm.

In this experiment, as exhibited in Fig. S1, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs shows maximum absorption peak at 352nm, and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs displays a typical excitation-dependent feature as showed in Fig. S2. These results confirm the successful preparation of desirable CDs. The diameter of CCDs was determined by an atomic force microscope (AFM) in the tapping mode. Fig. 1 displays the AFM image for a typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1), which shows that most of the CCDs were discrete, monodisperse, and tiny dots. The cross-sectional view of the CCDs by AFM given in Fig. 1 suggests that the average thickness of CDs is around 2nm.

Fig. 2 indicates the change of absorption for a typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1) under different concentration of Cu2+. It is known that Cyclam has superior affinity for Cu2+ and excellent water solubility, which have been successfully applied in selective recognition of Cu2+ in aqueous media [34,36]. In the process, a broad absorption band at 525nm was appeared upon successive titration of Cu2+ (Fig. 2A). In addition, with the constant addition of copper ion, the maximal absorbance at 525nm linearly grows up to a maximum value, as displayed in Fig. 2B, which was attributable to adequate complexation of Cyclam moieties with Cu2+ in a totally aqueous medium. The behavior is also reported that the Cyclam has been successfully covalently anchored on the surface of CDs and reveals a high sensitivity for Cu2+ [36]. Moreover, the grafted Cyclam content can be measured by spectrophotometric titration, which linked in the surface of nanoparticles [34,36], as showed in Table 1. In additional, as exhibited in Table 1 and Fig. S3, with the content of grafted Cyclam increased, the fluorescence emission intensity of prepared CCDs gradually decrease, which may be due to the possible passivation of the surface groups of CDs via two step chemical modification. Thus, suitable content of grafted Cyclam is of great importance for the prepared sensor to keep favorable sensing properties.

Fig. 2 indicates the change of absorption for a typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1) under different concentration of Cu2+. It is known that Cyclam has superior affinity for Cu2+ and excellent water solubility, which have been successfully applied in selective recognition of Cu2+ in aqueous media [34,36]. In the process, a broad absorption band at 525nm was appeared upon successive titration of Cu2+ (Fig. 2A). In addition, with the constant addition of copper ion, the maximal absorbance at 525nm linearly grows up to a maximum value, as displayed in Fig. 2B, which was attributable to adequate complexation of Cyclam moieties with Cu2+ in a totally aqueous medium. The behavior is also reported that the Cyclam has been successfully covalently anchored on the surface of CDs and reveals a high sensitivity for Cu2+ [36]. Moreover, the grafted Cyclam content can be measured by spectrophotometric titration, which linked in the surface of nanoparticles [34,36], as showed in Table 1. In additional, as exhibited in Table 1 and Fig. S3, with the content of grafted Cyclam increased, the fluorescence emission intensity of prepared CCDs gradually decrease, which may be due to the possible passivation of the surface groups of CDs via two step chemical modification. Thus, suitable content of grafted Cyclam is of great importance for the prepared sensor to keep favorable sensing properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective fluorescence recognition of Cu2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, with the increasing concentration of Cu2+, the fluorescence spectra of a diluted CCDs (CCD-3) dispersion was measured in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water, which was showed in Fig. 3A. Due to the effective binding Cu2+ with the CCDs (CCD-3), an obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity is observed in the presence of a tiny Cu2+, and the maximum quenching rate can reach 63% (Fig. 3A). For comparison, the fluorescence intensity is seldom changed for successive addition of water to the same CCDs sample (Fig. S4), and it also varied a little even at high Cu2+ concentrations for the un-functionalized CDs (Fig. S5). This apparently indicates that the complexation of Cu2+ with the CCDs can be led to the obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs. A linear plot of the typical sample (CCD-3) was obtained in the Cu2+ concentration range from 0.7μM to 4.0μM (Fig. 3B). The association constant K is 1.097×105 Lmol−1 and the correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.9966. Finally, the Stern–Volmer plot for binding of Cu2+ to the CCDs sample (CCD-3) has been done (Fig. S6) and the detection limit can be exactly calculated. So the estimated detection limit with this sensor is 100nM, which is well suitable for sensing copper ion in the blood system and drinking water.

In the experiment, with the increasing concentration of Cu2+, the fluorescence spectra of a diluted CCDs (CCD-3) dispersion was measured in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water, which was showed in Fig. 3A. Due to the effective binding Cu2+ with the CCDs (CCD-3), an obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity is observed in the presence of a tiny Cu2+, and the maximum quenching rate can reach 63% (Fig. 3A). For comparison, the fluorescence intensity is seldom changed for successive addition of water to the same CCDs sample (Fig. S4), and it also varied a little even at high Cu2+ concentrations for the un-functionalized CDs (Fig. S5). This apparently indicates that the complexation of Cu2+ with the CCDs can be led to the obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs. A linear plot of the typical sample (CCD-3) was obtained in the Cu2+ concentration range from 0.7μM to 4.0μM (Fig. 3B). The association constant K is 1.097×105 Lmol−1 and the correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.9966. Finally, the Stern–Volmer plot for binding of Cu2+ to the CCDs sample (CCD-3) has been done (Fig. S6) and the detection limit can be exactly calculated. So the estimated detection limit with this sensor is 100nM, which is well suitable for sensing copper ion in the blood system and drinking water.

Depending on the protocol of FRET [37], an effective energy transfer was mainly depended on two factors. The one is the donor's emission spectra and the absorption spectra of acceptor can overlap well. The other is the distance between the donor and acceptor is within the Förster effective radius (generally, 1–10nm). As showed in Fig. S7, the emission band of CDs was overlapped with the absorption band of Cyclam-Cu2+ complex, which means the FRET process can be possibly taken place between the CDs and the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex. To further investigate the occurrence of FRET between them, the decay curves of the CCDs sample (CCD-3) were compared before and after increasing Cu2+ into the dispersion, as showed in Fig. S8. When the absence of Cu2+ in this system, the estimated average lifetime of the sample at 460nm was 12.6ns, while after adding 20μM of Cu2+ to the CCDs solution, the average lifetime of CCDs became shorter (10.4ns at 460nm) than that of the CCDs without addition of Cu2+. This result significantly indicated the existing FRET process between the CDs and the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex.

Depending on the protocol of FRET [37], an effective energy transfer was mainly depended on two factors. The one is the donor's emission spectra and the absorption spectra of acceptor can overlap well. The other is the distance between the donor and acceptor is within the Förster effective radius (generally, 1–10nm). As showed in Fig. S7, the emission band of CDs was overlapped with the absorption band of Cyclam-Cu2+ complex, which means the FRET process can be possibly taken place between the CDs and the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex. To further investigate the occurrence of FRET between them, the decay curves of the CCDs sample (CCD-3) were compared before and after increasing Cu2+ into the dispersion, as showed in Fig. S8. When the absence of Cu2+ in this system, the estimated average lifetime of the sample at 460nm was 12.6ns, while after adding 20μM of Cu2+ to the CCDs solution, the average lifetime of CCDs became shorter (10.4ns at 460nm) than that of the CCDs without addition of Cu2+. This result significantly indicated the existing FRET process between the CDs and the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex.

As the data exhibited in Fig. S9 and Table 1, the effect of the amount of acceptor (the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex) on experimental energy transfer efficiency (E) and the initial fluorescence intensity has been investigated. As exhibited in Table 1, compared with un-functionalized CDs, the initial fluorescence intensity of CCDs was gradually decreased with the increasing of capped Cu2+-ligand on the surface. On the other hand, for the sample with relatively low content of Cyclam (CCD-1), the fluorescence intensity of CCDs can be quenched by Cu2+, and the E is 43% at 20μM of Cu2+ (Fig. S9 and Table 1). For comparison, the CCDs sample with higher amount of Cyclam (CCD-2, CCD-3), its E can be reached a relatively higher value (51% and 63%) at the same Cu2+ concentration. However, the further increase of Cyclam (CCD-4, 11.0mM) cannot substantially promote E (68%), but greatly weakens the initial fluorescence intensity of CCDs (Table 1). Thus, in order to achieve relatively high quenching efficiency and keep suitable fluorescence intensity, an appropriate amount of linked Cyclam should be ensured in this study.

As the data exhibited in Fig. S9 and Table 1, the effect of the amount of acceptor (the Cyclam-Cu2+ complex) on experimental energy transfer efficiency (E) and the initial fluorescence intensity has been investigated. As exhibited in Table 1, compared with un-functionalized CDs, the initial fluorescence intensity of CCDs was gradually decreased with the increasing of capped Cu2+-ligand on the surface. On the other hand, for the sample with relatively low content of Cyclam (CCD-1), the fluorescence intensity of CCDs can be quenched by Cu2+, and the E is 43% at 20μM of Cu2+ (Fig. S9 and Table 1). For comparison, the CCDs sample with higher amount of Cyclam (CCD-2, CCD-3), its E can be reached a relatively higher value (51% and 63%) at the same Cu2+ concentration. However, the further increase of Cyclam (CCD-4, 11.0mM) cannot substantially promote E (68%), but greatly weakens the initial fluorescence intensity of CCDs (Table 1). Thus, in order to achieve relatively high quenching efficiency and keep suitable fluorescence intensity, an appropriate amount of linked Cyclam should be ensured in this study.

To investigate the selectivity of the CCDs for Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CCDs sample (CCD-3) at 460nm was measured after introducing of various competitive and transition metal cations like Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+ (in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water). As listed in Fig. 4A, except the addition of Fe3+ leading to obvious fluorescence quenching, the other metal ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+ and Zn2+ show quite few effect on the fluorescent intensity of CCDs sample. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4B, the interference of coexisting ions for CCDs sensing Cu2+ was also exhibit similar effect for the recognition of Cu2+. In order to achieve the single detection of Cu2+, the efficient Fe3+ chelating reagent sodium hexametaphosphate (SHPP) was added to minimize the interference of Cu2+ [23]. As shown in Fig. S10, the introduction of Fe3+ into CCD sensor and Cu2+ mixtures had negligible influence on the detection of Cu2+ in the presence of SHPP. So Fe3+ can be successfully masked under these conditions with seldom effect on the Cu2+ detection, allowing for the high selective detection of Cu2+.

To investigate the selectivity of the CCDs for Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CCDs sample (CCD-3) at 460nm was measured after introducing of various competitive and transition metal cations like Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+ (in pH 7.4 HEPES buffered water). As listed in Fig. 4A, except the addition of Fe3+ leading to obvious fluorescence quenching, the other metal ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+ and Zn2+ show quite few effect on the fluorescent intensity of CCDs sample. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4B, the interference of coexisting ions for CCDs sensing Cu2+ was also exhibit similar effect for the recognition of Cu2+. In order to achieve the single detection of Cu2+, the efficient Fe3+ chelating reagent sodium hexametaphosphate (SHPP) was added to minimize the interference of Cu2+ [23]. As shown in Fig. S10, the introduction of Fe3+ into CCD sensor and Cu2+ mixtures had negligible influence on the detection of Cu2+ in the presence of SHPP. So Fe3+ can be successfully masked under these conditions with seldom effect on the Cu2+ detection, allowing for the high selective detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn-on sensing of S2− in water
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, S2− can combine with Cu2+ and form a stable species [2,4,34]. The appearance of S2− in the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble will lead to the release of free CCDs, which indicates the sensor will be a potential turn-on fluorescent sensor for S2− detection. Based on this opinion, some quantitative analysis of the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble toward S2− was explored by the fluorescence test [2,34]. As showed in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of the original CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble at 460nm is quite low, while after introduction of S2−, the fluorescence intensity of the ensemble was approximate linearly enhanced at pH 7.4 (Fig. 5B), and when the S2− concentration reach to 20μM, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced up to 3.0-fold, then, the fluorescence intensity increased slowly to maximum with the continued increase of S2−. Furthermore, it is a real good line relationship in the range of 0–15μM between the fluorescence intensity of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble and the concentration of S2− (Fig. 5B). In addition, the introduction of S2− to the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble also leads the recovery of the average lifetime (12.4ns) of the CCDs (Fig. S8). Meanwhile, the detection limit of the CCDs-Cu2+ complex system for the sensing of S2− can reach 130nM, which is obviously lower than the maximum recommended sulfide concentration (about 500μg/L or 15μM) in drinking water by World Health Organization [38,39]. Thus, this sensor can be well used for S2− sensing in water quality monitoring.

Notably, S2− can combine with Cu2+ and form a stable species [2,4,34]. The appearance of S2− in the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble will lead to the release of free CCDs, which indicates the sensor will be a potential turn-on fluorescent sensor for S2− detection. Based on this opinion, some quantitative analysis of the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble toward S2− was explored by the fluorescence test [2,34]. As showed in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of the original CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble at 460nm is quite low, while after introduction of S2−, the fluorescence intensity of the ensemble was approximate linearly enhanced at pH 7.4 (Fig. 5B), and when the S2− concentration reach to 20μM, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced up to 3.0-fold, then, the fluorescence intensity increased slowly to maximum with the continued increase of S2−. Furthermore, it is a real good line relationship in the range of 0–15μM between the fluorescence intensity of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble and the concentration of S2− (Fig. 5B). In addition, the introduction of S2− to the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble also leads the recovery of the average lifetime (12.4ns) of the CCDs (Fig. S8). Meanwhile, the detection limit of the CCDs-Cu2+ complex system for the sensing of S2− can reach 130nM, which is obviously lower than the maximum recommended sulfide concentration (about 500μg/L or 15μM) in drinking water by World Health Organization [38,39]. Thus, this sensor can be well used for S2− sensing in water quality monitoring.

To further study the selectivity of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble for recognizing S2−, the other familiar anions such as HCO3 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, F−, Br−, HPO4 −, ClO4 − have been used in the study for contrast. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6A that only the addition of S2− can lead to the significant fluorescence enhancement of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble, which displays excellent selectivity for sensing S2−. In order to further evaluate the anti-interference character of the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble toward S2−, the different coexisting anions were employed in the test. Obviously, coexistence of these anions displays little interference with the fluorescence enhancement of CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble (Fig. 6B). This suggests that the CCDs-Cu2+ complex ensemble can be served as a highly selective sensor for S2− with an attractive anti-interference character.

As displayed in Fig. S11, when alternative introducing of Cu2+ (20μM) and S2− (20μM), the typical CCDs sample (CCD-3) exhibits the reversibly switchable sensing nature. In this study, Cu2+ or S2− can be used to selectively turn-off or turn-on the fluorescence emission of the prepared CCDs respectively. Although the accumulated Cu2+ or S2− species can lead to the appearance of “fatigue” effects, notably fluorescent change still can be seen for sensing Cu2+ and S2− after four test cycles. Thus, the as-prepared sensors have attractive nature for renewable multifunctional fluorescent sensing in biological applications.

As displayed in Fig. S11, when alternative introducing of Cu2+ (20μM) and S2− (20μM), the typical CCDs sample (CCD-3) exhibits the reversibly switchable sensing nature. In this study, Cu2+ or S2− can be used to selectively turn-off or turn-on the fluorescence emission of the prepared CCDs respectively. Although the accumulated Cu2+ or S2− species can lead to the appearance of “fatigue” effects, notably fluorescent change still can be seen for sensing Cu2+ and S2− after four test cycles. Thus, the as-prepared sensors have attractive nature for renewable multifunctional fluorescent sensing in biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of pH-dependence and longterm photostability of senor
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that the broad pH applicable range is important for sensors in environmental or biological application. The pH effect on the CCDs sensor for sensing Cu2+ is given in Fig. S12. It can be noted that the pH values from 4.0 to 10.0 have a negligible influence on I/I 0 after addition of some Cu2+ ([Cu2+]=10μM). Therefore, the CCDs sensor could be used for detection of Cu2+ in a wide pH range under the physiological environment.

It is known that the broad pH applicable range is important for sensors in environmental or biological application. The pH effect on the CCDs sensor for sensing Cu2+ is given in Fig. S12. It can be noted that the pH values from 4.0 to 10.0 have a negligible influence on I/I 0 after addition of some Cu2+ ([Cu2+]=10μM). Therefore, the CCDs sensor could be used for detection of Cu2+ in a wide pH range under the physiological environment.

The longterm fluorescence stability of the CCDs dispersion for the recognition Cu2+ was also investigated. Following work has been done in this test. The original CCDs solution was stored in a sealed vessel and then the vessel was kept in the dark conditions under room temperature. Then an aliquot was picked out from the vessel for recognition Cu2+. Although the CCDs was stored more than 35 days, I/I 0 of the CCDs in the presence of Cu2+ (10μM) has a minor change not more than 2% through the research. The result reveals that the sensor possesses excellent longterm fluorescence stability for recognition Cu2+ (Fig. S13). In addition, we also evaluate the photostability of the CCDs sample. The CCDs solution was placed under a continuous 365nm UV lamp (12W) illumination, then we can achieve the result of the effect of irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1), which was displayed in Fig. S14. The fluorescence intensities of the sensor did not change a lot within the limited irradiation time (90min), which indicates the CCDs have favorable photostability. All the results demonstrate that CCDs can be used for a potential sensor for longterm tracking Cu2+ even in complex conditions.

The longterm fluorescence stability of the CCDs dispersion for the recognition Cu2+ was also investigated. Following work has been done in this test. The original CCDs solution was stored in a sealed vessel and then the vessel was kept in the dark conditions under room temperature. Then an aliquot was picked out from the vessel for recognition Cu2+. Although the CCDs was stored more than 35 days, I/I 0 of the CCDs in the presence of Cu2+ (10μM) has a minor change not more than 2% through the research. The result reveals that the sensor possesses excellent longterm fluorescence stability for recognition Cu2+ (Fig. S13). In addition, we also evaluate the photostability of the CCDs sample. The CCDs solution was placed under a continuous 365nm UV lamp (12W) illumination, then we can achieve the result of the effect of irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of typical CCDs sample (CCD-3, Table 1), which was displayed in Fig. S14. The fluorescence intensities of the sensor did not change a lot within the limited irradiation time (90min), which indicates the CCDs have favorable photostability. All the results demonstrate that CCDs can be used for a potential sensor for longterm tracking Cu2+ even in complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and the Imaging of copper ion and sulfide ion in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: For living cells imaging applications, the investigation of the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared sensor is necessary. The cytotoxicity of the prepared CCDs sensor was studied via using the HeLa cells line via the MTT assay according to ISO 10993-5. The results are presented in Fig. S15. It obviously displays that the prepared CCDs sensor possess high cell viability (>90%) even though the concentration of CCDs was added up to 300μgml−1. All the results indicate that the prepared CCDs sensor has very low cytotoxicity and is favorable for fluorescence recognition of Cu2+ and S2− in live cells.

For living cells imaging applications, the investigation of the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared sensor is necessary. The cytotoxicity of the prepared CCDs sensor was studied via using the HeLa cells line via the MTT assay according to ISO 10993-5. The results are presented in Fig. S15. It obviously displays that the prepared CCDs sensor possess high cell viability (>90%) even though the concentration of CCDs was added up to 300μgml−1. All the results indicate that the prepared CCDs sensor has very low cytotoxicity and is favorable for fluorescence recognition of Cu2+ and S2− in live cells.

Moreover, the monitoring of Cu2+ and S2− via fluorescence imaging of the CCDs in living cells was also evaluated. For the tracking of Cu2+, the HeLa (human cervical cancer) cells were incubated with the suitable concentration of CCDs (50μgml−1) for 30min, and then treated with different concentration of Cu2+ for 1h at 37°C. As shown in Fig. 7 , compared with the blank CCDs-stained cells (A and D), the intracellular fluorescence turn to weaker after being treated with 50μM of Cu2+ (B and E), and nearly disappears after loaded with 100μM of Cu2+ (C and F). Furthermore, as similar to above fluorescence titration experiments, the CCDs/Cu2+ complex ensemble can also be used to track S2− in live cells. Fig. 8 shows confocal images for the HeLa cells incubated with the ensemble before and after dealt with S2−. Herein, HeLa cells were loaded with 50μgml−1 of CCDs and 100μM of Cu2+ for 1.5h (A and D), then, ensemble-stained cells were exposed to 75μM (B and E) and 120μM of S2− (C and F) for 30min. As can be seen in Fig. 8, with the increasing of S2− in cells, the blue fluorescence enhanced considerably (from A–C or D–F). All the results demonstrate that the CCDs or its ensemble can be adapted to monitor Cu2+ or S2− level change inside live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test the practicability of sensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further test the practicability of the developed sensor, the CCD-3 sample was used to detect the concentrations of Cu2+ ions in several tap water and blood serum samples. As exhibited in Table 2 , the RSD for all assays are less than 0.38%, and recoveries are in the range of 85% to 99.5%. These results demonstrated the good precision accuracy of the present method for the recognition of Cu2+ ions in real aqueous samples.

In summary, we developed a highly selective and sensitive sensor for recognition of Cu2+ and subsequent S2− in a totally aqueous solution by surface modification of CDs. The synthesis strategy is facile, low cost, and environment-friendly. In this system, fluorescent CDs act as the donor and the Cyclam served as the potential acceptor linked on the surface of CDs. FRET can be effectively taken place between them after introducing of Cu2+. The as-prepared CCDs can be used to sensitively recognize Cu2+ as down to 100nM. Meanwhile, prepared CCDs sensor displays high anti-interference performance against 11 kinds of coexisting cations. On the other hand, considering the high affinity of Cu2+ with S2−, the ensemble of CCDs with suitable concentration of Cu2+ can also be served as a highly sensitive (detection limit: 130nM) and selective probe for S2−. In additional, the prepared CCDs sensor also exhibited reversible switching property for sensing Cu2+ and S2−, a wide pH applicable range, outstanding longterm fluorescence stability (>35 days), as well as excellent photostability. Moreover, the prepared CCDs sensor with quite low cytotoxicity can also be used for monitoring Cu2+ and S2− inside live cells and recognition of Cu2+ ions in real aqueous samples. This approach provides some meaningful insights for achieving technically-simple multifunctional detection for additional analytes in environmental and biological fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support of the present work by NSFC (Project nos. 51373002, 21405040, 21172066), Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (Project no. 12B041), Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Project nos. 2014M550418, 2015T80867), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (Project no. 2013008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.10.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dot-loaded alginate gels as recoverable probes: fabrication and mechanism of fluorescent detection
Carbon-dot-based fluorescent turn-on sensor for selectively detecting sulfide anions in totally aqueous media and imaging inside live cells
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
Copper oxide based H2S dosimeters – modeling of percolation and diffusion processes
In situ synthesis of CuS nanotubes on Cu electrode for sensitive nonenzymatic glucose sensor
Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off-on” detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution
One-pot fabrication of polymer nanoparticle-based chemosensors for Cu2+ detection in aqueous media
l-cysteine-capped ZnS quantum dots based fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ ion
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Highly selective electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) for sulfide ion determination at multi-wall carbon nanotubes-modified graphite electrode
A robust, water-soluble and low cytotoxic fluorescent probe for sulfide anion achieved through incorporation of betaine
Rapid and highly selective relay recognition of Cu(II) and sulfide ions by a simple benzimidazole-based fluorescent sensor in water
A retrievable and highly selective fluorescent sensor for detecting copper and sulfide
Development of novel and sensitive methods for the determination of sulfide in aqueous samples by hydrogen sulfide generation-inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy
Electrochemical determination of hydrogen sulfide at carbon nanotube modified electrodes
Brain hydrogen sulfide isseverely decreased in Alzheimer's disease
New electro-chemical sensor for the detection of hydrogen sulfide and other redox activespecies
Development of novel and sensi-tive methods for the determination of sulfide in aqueous samples by hydrogensulfide generation-inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy
Ion chromatographicdetermination of sulfide and cyanide in real matrices by using pulsed ampero-metric detection on a silver electrode
Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off-on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk – natural proteins
Polymer nanodots of graphitic carbon nitride as effective fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions
Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon nanodots through an eggshell membrane and their fluorescent application
Carbon nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting mercury ions in aqueous media and living cells
Graphene quantum dots/l-cysteine coreactant electrochemiluminescence system and its application in sensing lead(II) ions
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+
Preparation of europium complex-conjugated carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of copper(II) ions
A two-photon “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on carbon nanodots for imaging and selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Novel fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles for highly selective recognition of copper ion and sulfide anion in water
A general chemical conversion route to synthesize various ZnO-based core/shell structures
Fluorescence quenching upon binding of copper ions in dye-doped and ligand-capped polymer nanoparticles: a simple way to probe the dye accessibility in nano-sized templates
The third gas: H2S regulates perfusion pressure in both the isolated and perfused normal rat liver and in cirrhosis
Sensitive and selective detection of H2S and application in the presence of toluene, benzene, and xylene

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jian Chen is currently an associate professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include fluorescent nanomaterials, energy transfer in colloides, chemosensors and biosensors. He has published more than 20 papers in Small, Chemical Communications, Macromolecules, Polymer Chemistry, Nanotechnology, Journal of Physical Chemistry B, and so on.

Ya Li received her bachelor's degree in 2013 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors and biosensors. She has publised one paper in Sensors and Actuators: B Chemical.

Kun Lv received her bachelor's degree in 2014 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors.

Weibang Zhong received her bachelor's degree in 2014 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching fluorescent nanomaterials. He has publised one paper in Macromolecules.
Hong Wang is currently a junior in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors.

Zhan Wu is currently a lecturer in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, China. Her primary research interests include biosensing and chemometrics. She has published more than 20 papers in Journal of the American Chemical Society, Acs Nano, Analytical Chemistry, and so on.

Pinggui Yi is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include functional molecules and materials, proton transfer, chemosensors and biosensors. He has published more than 60 papers in Journal of Physical Chemistry B, Chemical Physics Letters, The Journal of Chemical Physics, and so on.

Jianhui Jiang is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, China. His primary research interests include biosensing and chemometrics. He has published more than 100 papers in Journal of the American Chemical Society, Angewandte Chemie-International Edition, Acs Nano, Analytical Chemistry, Chemical communications, Small, and so on.
